chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number:
VIST 487/CSCE 443
Course Title:
GAME DEVELOPMENT
Section:
502
Time:
Tuesdays & Thursdays 9:35 am - 11:15 am
Location:
Langford Architecture Building C 109A
Credit Hours:
3 (2 lecture hours & 2 lab hours)

chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Instructor:
Mason Smith '15, Instructional Assistant Professor
Office:
Langford Architecture Building A 132
E-Mail:
masonsmith@tamu.edu
Office Hours:
By appointment

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content:
Aesthetic and technical aspects of computer game development, including game mechanics, story
development, content creation and game programming; includes game design, interface design, 3D
modeling and animation, graphics algorithms, shader programming and artificial intelligence; group
project includes the design and development of a game from start to finish.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Grade of C or better in VIST 386 or CSCE 441, or approval of instructor.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
• Demonstrate a comprehensive understanding of the digital game development and production
pipeline, from initial ideation to public release.
• Develop and present a detailed Game Design Document (GDD) outlining the design of a digital game.
• Communicate the core design concepts of their game effectively to peers, instructors, and industry
professionals.
• Create and manage a production schedule to ensure timely achievement of development milestones.
• Contribute to various roles within the digital game development process.
• Apply industry-standard practices and methodologies in the development of digital games.
• Focus on critical development elements that support the game's core design, including prototypes,
vertical slices, demos, and development builds.
• Deliver a polished, portfolio-quality or professional-grade final build of their team's game, suitable for
public distribution.
• Analyze and showcase their game's gameplay elements in a compelling video trailer.
• Establish a public presence for their games via release and distribution on digital platforms and
submitting it to events, festivals, competitions, and conferences.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
There is no required textbook for this course. All needed course materials will be provided by the
instructor on Canvas.

There are workstations in the Langford C 109A lab that should have all the software you need to
complete this course, and you have the added benefit of support from Technology Services. If you plan on
using your personal laptop, you assume full responsibility for its function, performance, and capacity.

Your laptop must be able to connect with displays in the studio.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
•
Final grade rubric:
Ο
A = 90 - 100: Team delivered an engaging, polished game with deliverables that
exceeded expectations
Ο
B = 80 - 89: Team delivered an engaging, complete game with deliverables that met
expectations
Ο
C = 70 - 79: Team delivered a game with deliverables that did not quite meet
expectations
Ο
D = 60 - 69: Team delivered an incomplete game with deliverables and/or failed to meet
expectations
Ο
F = below 60: Team delivered an unplayable and/or unfinished game and/or deliverables
•
Course graded items:
Ο Game pitch *
10 points
Ο
Milestone 1*
10 points
Ο
Milestone 2*
10 points
Ο
Milestone 3*
10 points
Ο
Final game deliverables *
•
itch.io game page *
•
Game trailer *
10 points
• Game screenshots *
10 points
• Game page content *
10 points
•
Downloadable game
*
20 points
Ο
Discord Discussions (x 5)
10 points

Total
100 points

* For these graded items, your team shares the grade (for exceptions, see the section on Participation below)

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
•
Assignments submitted to Canvas after the established due date constitutes late work.
•
Late work submitted within one week of the assignment due date gets an automatic 10% grade
deduction for that assignment.
•
I will not accept late work submitted more than 1 week after the assignment due date.

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is
exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Participation
chunks.content:
Students are expected to participate in course lectures and studio time. Students are expected to conduct
themselves in a professional manner and give due respect to the instructors and their peers.

Teams are encouraged to track the participation and effort level of their individual team members in
whatever way they choose. Individual team members who are consistently absent from class activities
and/or studio time, do not put forth a reasonable effort, or otherwise prove toxic or detrimental to the
success of their team project will see their final grade suffer.
